one_line_takeaway: DePrompt fine-tunes a local LLM to recognize prompt PII and then generatively substitutes identifiers and adversarially perturbs sensitive attributes, trading a small amount of raw privacy leakage for markedly better downstream prompt utility than deletion/masking/generalization baselines.
key_finding: On privacy-vs-utility, DePrompt does NOT achieve the strongest raw privacy protection but wins on the balance. In the desensitization comparison (Table 3, K=3, N=5), Deletion gave the lowest PII-extraction rate epsilon_e=0.046 while DePrompt was 0.097; but DePrompt achieved the best utility by a wide margin: Inference Loss similarity 0.96 (vs 0.64 Generalization, 0.18 Deletion) and lowest readability perplexity RL=2.30 (vs 4.28 Generalization, 12.04 Deletion). On PII entity recognition (Table 2) DePrompt averaged 95.95% accuracy, narrowly ahead of GPT-3.5-turbo (95.33%) and Llama3 (94.91%) and well ahead of BERT_LSTM_CRF (88.05%) and BERT_CRF (83.64%). Scene recognition overall accuracy was 98% (100% in medical).
core_contribution: DePrompt is a locally-deployable framework for desensitizing Personally Identifiable Information (PII) in LLM prompts at inference time, while preserving prompt utility. Its own three contributions are: (1) a fine-tuned LLM (LoRA) that performs scene recognition and context-aware PII entity extraction using dynamic privacy-concept templates, reporting 95.95% average recognition accuracy; (2) an 'adversarial generative desensitization' method that, unlike prior work, does not simply delete/mask entities but generatively substitutes identifiers and perturbs confidential attributes with special characters (e.g. 'schistosomiasis' -> 'sch¡stosom¡@s¡s') so NER tools fail to extract them while the target LLM can still infer them, and concatenates/shuffles N candidate prompts to confuse an eavesdropper; (3) a prompt-specific privacy-and-utility evaluation metric suite. The authors explicitly distinguish their inference-stage, per-prompt approach from prior differential-privacy prompt work (DP-OPT by Hong et al. 2024; Yu et al. 2024), which they characterize as operating on whole datasets during fine-tuning and thus unsuited to protecting individual prompt instances.
threat_model: The LLM cloud service provider (CSP) is honest-but-curious (semi-honest): it faithfully processes prompts and does not maliciously alter them or responses, but is curious about their contents. The communication channel between the local environment and the cloud is assumed to be monitorable, so adversaries can observe the prompts a user U submits to the CSP as well as the LLM frameworks/interfaces used. Adversaries are assumed to have white-box background knowledge and try to extract and analyze personal sensitive information for downstream tasks such as user profiling. Two concrete attacks are formalized to measure risk: a PII Extraction attack (attacker maximally extracts PII from the desensitized prompt) and an Identifier Linkage attack (attacker links leaked private attributes back to a specific individual using a list of direct identifiers, |D| set to 5).
limitations: (1) DePrompt's raw privacy leakage is worse than simple Deletion/Masking (epsilon_e 0.097 vs 0.046-0.049); the authors argue their adversarial generation still lowers effective risk by making attackers unable to verify attribute veracity, but this is asserted rather than measured against an adaptive attacker who knows the perturbation scheme. (2) Security rests on the assumption that the CSP LLM can still correctly infer over adversarially-perturbed text while NER tools cannot — robustness of this gap to stronger extractors is not stressed. (3) The recognition margin over GPT-3.5-turbo/Llama3 is small (~0.6-1 pt). (4) Datasets are self-collected/manually-annotated subsets (1000 per scene; 1400 PII instances, >=200 per type), limiting generalizability, and medical-payment entities were the weakest (PAYMENT 91.13%). (5) An internal inconsistency: the setup names llama-2 as the fine-tuned base, while Table 2 reports Llama3 and Qwen2 rows. (6) The framework's overhead (N-fold prompt concatenation sent to a paid API) and its cost/latency are not quantified.

datasets_used: HC3 (Human-ChatGPT Comparison Corpus; ~24K questions, 59K human answers, 27K ChatGPT answers)
Text Anonymization Benchmark / TAB (Pilan et al. 2022, used as external benchmark)
Self-collected subsets: 1000 prompts per scene (medical/daily/financial) for scene recognition; 1400 manually-annotated PII instances (>=200 per entity type) for private-entity extraction
quantitative_results: PII entity recognition average accuracy: DePrompt 95.95% (Table 2)
Per-entity DePrompt accuracy: PERSON 98.88, CODE 98.73, CONTACT 99.56, HEALTH 92.23, MEDICAL 92.56, PAYMENT 91.13, ASSET 98.56
Desensitization Table 3 (Ours, K=3 N=5): epsilon_e PII-extraction=0.097, epsilon_i identifier-linkage=0.24, Semantic Loss=0.92, Inference Loss=0.96, Readability(PPL)=2.30
Table 3 Deletion: epsilon_e=0.046, epsilon_i=0.21, SL=0.76, IL=0.18, RL=12.04
Table 3 Tokenization: epsilon_e=0.052, epsilon_i=0.22, SL=0.88, IL=0.22, RL=7.64
Table 3 Masking: epsilon_e=0.049, epsilon_i=0.21, SL=0.72, IL=0.17, RL=9.37
Table 3 Generalization: epsilon_e=0.124, epsilon_i=0.67, SL=0.82, IL=0.64, RL=4.28
baselines_compared: Entity/scene recognition: BERT, BERT_LSTM, BERT_CRF (83.64% avg), BERT_LSTM_CRF (88.05% avg), Llama3 (94.91% avg), Qwen2 (93.62% avg), GPT-3.5-turbo (95.33% avg)
Benchmark (TAB) methods: Neural NER (RoBERTa), Presidio (default), Presidio (+ORG)
Desensitization methods: Deletion, Tokenization, Masking, Generalization
Positioned against but not empirically compared: DP-OPT (Hong et al. 2024) and Yu et al. 2024 differential-privacy prompt schemes
